title: The LC oscillator: the current is the slope of the charge
scenario: A capacitor $C = 100\ \mu$F is charged to $Q_0 = 0.01$ C and then connected across an inductor $L = 10$ mH. With no resistance to drain it, the charge $Q(t)$ and the current $I(t)$ oscillate forever at angular frequency $\omega = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. Plotted as a stacked $Q$–$t$ over $I$–$t$, the slope of the charge *is* the current at every instant — the same slope↔value pivot as position and velocity, and the electrical twin of a mass on a spring. Drag $L$ and watch the period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$ stretch; the energy sloshes between the capacitor and the inductor while the total stays fixed.

assumptions.claim: An ideal LC circuit: a perfect inductor and capacitor with no resistance, so no energy is dissipated and the oscillation never decays.
The capacitor starts fully charged to $Q_0$ and the initial current is zero, $I(0) = 0$ — all the energy begins in the capacitor's electric field.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (a ringing circuit and a period)
steps.prose: An LC circuit oscillates: with no resistance to dissipate energy, the charge and current swing back and forth forever at angular frequency $\omega = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$. You are handed the period as a rule — the same form as the spring's $T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ — with no account of *why* the circuit rings or where $\omega = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ comes from.

steps.label: Kirchhoff's voltage law is a second-order differential equation
steps.prose: Around the loop, the inductor's back-EMF $L\,dI/dt$ balances the capacitor voltage $Q/C$. Because the current is the rate of change of charge, $I = dQ/dt$, this is a *second-order* differential equation for $Q(t)$ — the exact shape of the mass–spring equation $x'' = -\omega^2 x$, with $\omega^2 = 1/(LC)$.

steps.label: Solving it gives the oscillation; its slope is the current
steps.prose: The solution is a cosine at $\omega = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ — so $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$ falls straight out of the equation. Differentiating the charge gives the current: it lags a quarter cycle behind, peaking when the charge is zero. The current is the *slope* of the charge.

steps.label: Watch the two panels — I is the slope of Q, and energy sloshes
steps.prose: Drag $L$ or $Q_0$: the charge $Q$ and current $I$ oscillate, and at every instant the slope of the upper $Q$–$t$ curve is the value of the lower $I$–$t$ curve — the same relationship as position and velocity, in a circuit. The energy trades between the capacitor ($Q^2/2C$, full when $Q$ peaks) and the inductor ($\tfrac12 LI^2$, full when $I$ peaks) while the total stays fixed. Raising $L$ stretches the period $2\pi\sqrt{LC}$.

proof.heading: The current is the slope of the charge, and the charge solves the LC equation — the mass–spring oscillator, one domain over.
proof.detail: check $I = dQ/dt$; check $L\,Q'' + Q/C = 0$; energy $\tfrac{Q^2}{2C} + \tfrac12 LI^2$ constant; $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$; quarter-period $90^\circ$ phase

checks.claim: The current is the slope of the charge: $I = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ — exactly as velocity is the slope of position.
The charge solves the LC equation $L\,\dfrac{d^2Q}{dt^2} + \dfrac{Q}{C} = 0$ — the electrical twin of the spring's $x'' = -\omega^2 x$.
Energy trades between the capacitor $\tfrac{Q^2}{2C}$ and the inductor $\tfrac12 LI^2$; the total stays at the initial $\tfrac{Q_0^2}{2C}$.
The period is $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$: a full cycle later the charge repeats, $Q(t+T) = Q(t)$.
A quarter period in, the capacitor is empty ($Q = 0$) while the current peaks — charge and current are $90^\circ$ out of phase.

misconception.claim: The charge and current both start large and fade away, the way they do when a capacitor charges through a resistor (RC).
misconception.refuted_by: Not here. With no resistor there is nothing to dissipate the energy, so nothing decays — the charge and current oscillate forever. They are $90^\circ$ out of phase: when the charge $Q$ is at its peak the current $I$ is zero (the capacitor is full and momentarily not flowing), and a quarter cycle later $Q = 0$ while $I$ is at its peak (the capacitor is empty and the current is greatest). The energy trades back and forth between the capacitor's $Q^2/2C$ and the inductor's $\tfrac12 LI^2$, but the total is constant. RC *relaxes*; LC *rings*.

practice.prompt: The capacitor ($C = 100\ \mu$F) and inductor ($L = 10$ mH) ring as an LC circuit. What is the period $T$ of the oscillation?

choices.misconception: Used $T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/C}$ — the *ratio* $L/C$ in place of the *product* $LC$. The period depends on $LC$ (the same product that sets $\omega = 1/\sqrt{LC}$), not $L/C$.
Reported the frequency $f = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ in hertz, not the period $T$ in seconds — the two are reciprocals.

practice.asks: the current at the instant of maximum charge
practice.prompt: At the instant the charge $Q$ reaches its maximum, what is the current $I$?

choices.misconception: Assumed the current peaks together with the charge. It is the opposite: $I = dQ/dt$, so the current is *zero* exactly when the charge is at a maximum (zero slope of $Q$), and largest when the charge crosses zero.
